messages.content: What is an atom?
An atom is the smallest unit of a chemical element. It consists of a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons.
What is the speed of light?
Approximately 299,792,458 metres per second (about 300,000 km/s) in a vacuum.
What is temperature?
A measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance — essentially, how hot or cold something is.
What are the three states of matter?
Solid, liquid, and gas. Some also include plasma as a fourth state.
What is Newton's first law of motion?
An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion, unless acted upon by an external force.

What is gravity?
Gravity is a fundamental force of nature that attracts objects with mass toward each other. On Earth, it pulls objects toward the ground with an acceleration of about 9.8 m/s².

What happens when you mix acid and base?
They neutralize each other in a reaction called neutralization, typically producing water and a salt.
What is the difference between mass and weight?
Mass is the amount of matter in an object (measured in kg). Weight is the force of gravity on that mass (measured in Newtons). Your mass is the same on Earth and the Moon; your weight is not.

What is gravity?
Gravity is a fundamental force of nature that attracts objects with mass toward each other. On Earth, it pulls objects toward the ground with an acceleration of about 9.8 m/s².
What is an atom?
An atom is the smallest unit of a chemical element. It consists of a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons.
What is the atomic number of carbon?
6. Carbon has 6 protons in its nucleus.
What is the freezing point of water?
0°C (32°F) at standard atmospheric pressure.

What is the difference between mass and weight?
Mass is the amount of matter in an object (measured in kg). Weight is the force of gravity on that mass (measured in Newtons). Your mass is the same on Earth and the Moon; your weight is not.

What is the periodic table?
A table that organizes all known chemical elements by their atomic number, electron configuration, and chemical properties.
